_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.1: This field asks for the municipal office or citizen service center where you want to submit this form. Write the name of your local city/ward office (市役所/区役所) or citizen service counter (市民総合窓口) in hiragana as shown in the field label.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.1: This section lists contact information for citizen consultation counters at various ward offices. If you have questions about the form or need assistance, contact the appropriate ward office based on your residential area.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.3: This column shows the phone numbers for each ward's citizen consultation counter. Call the number corresponding to your ward if you need help completing the form or have other municipal service questions.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.1: This refers to income tax return filing. If you have income in Japan, you may need to file a tax return (確定申告) by March 15th each year.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.3: This indicates that all people, including foreign residents, must enroll in health insurance. There are no exceptions based on nationality.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.9: This refers to the calculation of your health insurance premiums. The amount is automatically calculated based on your reported income.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.10: This is another reference to income tax returns. Make sure to file your tax return properly as it affects your health insurance calculations.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.12: This is informational text explaining that insurance premiums are calculated based on your previous year's income, so don't forget to file your income declaration. No action needed here.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.13: This refers to hospitals/medical institutions. You can use your health insurance card at hospitals and clinics to receive medical care at reduced cost.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.1: This refers to hospitals in general - no need to write anything specific here, it's explaining that you can receive medical care at hospitals when you have health insurance.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.9: This is emphasizing that national health insurance is very important - this is informational text, not a field to fill out.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.11: Again referring to hospitals in general as part of the explanation about health insurance coverage - no specific information needed from you here.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.13: This is a section header explaining 'When receiving medical treatment at a hospital or clinic' - it introduces the topic but doesn't require you to write anything.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.15: This mentions hospitals as part of instructions about medical care - it's explanatory text about where you can receive treatment, not a field requiring your input.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 3:.1: Write the name of the hospital or medical facility where you plan to receive treatment. Use the official Japanese name of the institution as it appears on their signage or documents.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 3:.15: This is an informational text explaining benefits of the national health insurance system - no action needed. It describes how you won't need to pay medical costs exceeding the prescribed limit amount when using your insurance properly.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 4:.3: This is an informational statement about the benefit of the medical expense limit system - you don't need to write anything here. It's explaining that you won't have to pay medical costs above a certain fixed amount.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 4:.8: This is an informational statement explaining that foreigners staying in Japan for more than 3 months must register in the resident registry (juminhyo) - no action required from you here. It's simply stating the requirement that leads to mandatory health insurance enrollment.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 5:.9: This is informational text about receiving annual health screenings to prevent lifestyle-related diseases - no action needed from you as it's explaining the benefit.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 5:.12: This appears to be explaining timing ('every year, mid-month') - likely informational about when screenings are available rather than requiring you to fill anything in.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 5:.15: This is informational text explaining that health screening vouchers are automatically sent to eligible residents around mid-May each year - no action required from you.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 6:.3: This appears to be part of a larger sentence about annual timing. Based on the context, you would write the month when you typically receive or need health checkup vouchers, which is usually May (5月) according to the form.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 6:.6: This is an informational statement, not a field to fill out. It explains that health checkup vouchers (for specific health examinations) are sent to eligible recipients around mid-May each year. No action needed from you.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 6:.2: This indicates the Health Support Section (健康支援課) contact information. This is referencing the department you should contact at TEL 043-238-9926 if you need assistance with health support services or have questions about the form.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 7:.3: This is an explanatory note defining what a 'Specific Health Checkup' means - it's a screening for lifestyle-related diseases like diabetes, high blood pressure, and abnormal lipid levels. No action needed as this is just informational text.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 7:.5: This completes the definition from field 3, explaining that the checkup is to investigate whether you have developed these lifestyle-related diseases. This is also informational text that requires no input from you.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 7:.2: Write the contact information where you can be reached for questions about this application, including phone number and preferably an address where correspondence can be sent.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 7:.3: This is an informational field explaining what overseas medical expense reimbursement covers - no input needed, just read to understand that this benefit applies when you received medical treatment at a foreign hospital for illness or injury.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 8:.5: Write the contact information (phone number, email, or address) where you can be reached if there are questions about your application. Make sure this information is current and accurate.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 8:.6: This is an informational field explaining what overseas medical expense reimbursement is - no writing required. It describes the benefit available when you receive medical treatment abroad and want partial reimbursement from Japanese health insurance.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 10.13: This explains that insurance premiums help pay for hospital costs when you get sick or injured. No action needed - this is informational text about how the insurance system works.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 10.15: This is a warning that using other people's information is a crime. No action needed - this is just an important reminder to only use your own personal information when filling out forms.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 10.2: This is informational text explaining that late payments will result in higher fees and penalties. No input is required - this is just a warning about payment delays affecting salary and savings.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 10.6: Write the name of the hospital or medical facility where you received treatment. Use the official name as it appears on your medical bills or receipts.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 11.3: Write the name of the hospital or medical facility where you received treatment. Use the official name as it appears on your medical records or receipts.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 11.7: This is informational text warning about criminal penalties - no input required. It explains that misusing insurance can constitute fraud punishable by up to 10 years imprisonment.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 11.11: This is continuation of the warning text about fraud penalties - no input required. It emphasizes the serious legal consequences of insurance fraud including potential 10-year prison sentences.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 11.2: This is a warning notice, not a field to fill out. It states that late fees will be charged if payment is delayed, so make sure to pay any required amounts by the deadline to avoid additional charges.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 12.1: This is an informational note warning that late payment fees will be incurred if payments are delayed. No action needed - this is just explaining consequences of late payment.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 12.9: This is a warning statement about fraud penalties being serious crimes. No action needed - this is informational text warning against providing false information on the form.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 12.12: This explains that paying insurance premiums through this method allows you to complete the procedure in one step. No action needed - this is describing a benefit of the payment process.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 12.13: This is a footnote definition explaining that imprisonment means entering prison and working. No action needed - this is just a clarification of legal terminology used elsewhere in the document.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.7: This text appears to be part of a definition explaining which types of livelihood protection benefits are excluded from the standard amount calculation. You don't need to fill anything here - it's explanatory text defining what components are removed when calculating the basic livelihood protection standard.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.12: This shows the formula result for the expected shortage ratio as a percentage. This field will be automatically calculated based on the income and expense figures you provide in other parts of the form - you typically don't need to manually enter this value.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.4: This appears to be the end of item (5) which relates to those determined eligible for collection deferrals under national health insurance. You would check this if you've been officially notified by the government that you qualify for payment deferrals due to emergency circumstances covered by the health insurance system.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.2: This row shows that if your income shortage is between 0-25% of what you need, you qualify for a 25% reduction in national health insurance premiums.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.3: This indicates that if your income shortage is between 25-50% of what you need, you qualify for a 50% reduction in national health insurance premiums.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.4: This shows that if your income shortage is between 50-75% of what you need, you qualify for a 75% reduction in national health insurance premiums.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.9: This is a legal reference explaining the mayor's authority to investigate and request additional documents from you or other agencies if needed to verify your application.

_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.11: This describes a condition where collection deferment (payment delay) is no longer needed because the person's financial situation has improved or changed. As a foreign resident, if your income or assets have increased significantly since receiving deferment, you would need to report this change and may be required to resume normal payments.

_vision_explanations:Page 4 - Section 1:.1: This is Article 14 stating that additional necessary matters not covered in these guidelines will be determined separately by the Director of the Health and Welfare Bureau. This is an informational clause - no action is required from you as an applicant.
_vision_explanations:Page 4 - Section 1:.2: This marks the beginning of the 'Supplementary Provisions' section which contains implementation dates and transitional measures. This is informational text showing when different versions of these guidelines became effective - no input is required from you.

_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 1:.3: This section shows the certification criteria for loan reduction/exemption. You don't need to write anything here - it's informational text explaining that your average monthly income must be within 115.5% of basic living expenses and savings must be 3 months or less of basic living expenses.
_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 1:.8: This appears to be part of a longer sentence describing eligibility conditions. You don't fill this out - it's explaining that severe disability or major asset damage of 50% or more qualifies you for this program.

_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 1:.1: Compare your current monthly income with your average monthly income from the previous year to determine the percentage decrease. This comparison forms the basis for demonstrating income reduction.
_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 1:.2: Calculate and write the percentage by which your income has decreased (e.g., if income dropped from 300,000 yen to 150,000 yen, write '50%'). The reduction must be 50% or more to qualify for this exemption.

_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 2:.9: Compare your current monthly income with your average monthly income from the 3 months before the income reduction occurred. This establishes the baseline for calculating your income decrease.
_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 2:.10: Calculate and enter the percentage by which your income has decreased from the baseline period. For example, if your income dropped from 300,000 yen to 150,000 yen, the decrease rate would be 50%.

_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.8: This is the form name column header - no action required, it's already filled in by the system.
_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.11: This indicates Form Number 1 - no action required, it's a reference number for the application form type.

_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.1: This indicates Form No. 2 - you don't need to write anything here as it's just a form identifier.

_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 3:.15: This indicates Form No. 13 for national health insurance premium reduction/exemption applications when receiving medical benefits. You don't need to write anything here as it's a form identifier.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.1: This section lists various life situations when you need to take action regarding National Health Insurance. You don't fill anything out here - just find your situation (like moving to Chiba City, getting a job, having a baby, etc.) to know what documents you need.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.3: This indicates the different insurance systems available. As a foreign resident, you'll typically be enrolled in the National Health Insurance system unless you have employer-sponsored health insurance.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.15: This explains that insurance premiums should be paid by automatic bank transfer from your account. Set up automatic payment at your bank to avoid missing payments and potential coverage issues.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.2: This is a QR code that you scan with your smartphone or mobile device. It will direct you to multilingual information about Japan's national health insurance system available in Japanese, English, Chinese, Korean, Spanish, Vietnamese, and other languages to help foreign residents understand the system better.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.1: This field is for a two-dimensional code (QR code or similar barcode). You typically don't need to write anything here - this section is usually pre-printed on official forms or generated automatically by the system when processing your application.

_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.5: This is just informational text identifying the mascot character 'Chi-ko-chan' for Chiba's national health insurance system. No action is required - this is simply a label for the cute panda character illustration on the page.

_vision_explanations:Page 4 - Section 1:.2: This is an informational note indicating that details about human and brain dock subsidies will be published in the April municipal newsletter (see page 27 for reference). No action needed - just note where to find more information.
_vision_explanations:Page 4 - Section 1:.5: This indicates the start date for submitting health examination results received at your own expense (see page 29 for details). Check page 29 for specific dates and procedures.
_vision_explanations:Page 4 - Section 1:.11: This refers to medical expenses [C] that are paid by insurance companies to medical institutions through the National Health Insurance Federation. This is part of the payment flow diagram - no action needed from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 4 - Section 1:.12: This represents the National Health Insurance Federation, which is an intermediary organization that processes insurance payments between insurers and medical institutions. This is informational only in the payment flow diagram.
_vision_explanations:Page 4 - Section 1:.14: This note states 'The timing is approximate.' It's a disclaimer indicating that all the dates and schedules mentioned in the document are estimates and may vary slightly.

_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.1: This is informational text advising you to avoid medical visits on holidays/weekends and duplicate consultations at multiple hospitals to reduce costs. No writing required - just read and follow this guidance.
_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.2: This appears to be part of a longer sentence about medical care content. You would fill in relevant information about medical treatments or services you're inquiring about or have received.
_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.4: This appears to be the end of a sentence stating something is 'necessary for each case.' Fill in what specific requirement or document is needed depending on the context of the preceding text.

_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.1: This section explains copayment rates for people aged 70-75. You don't need to write anything here - it's informational text showing that your copayment percentage (20% or 30%) depends on your income level as determined by your municipal tax assessment.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.2: This refers to the copayment rate table showing 30% for higher-income earners. No action needed - this is reference information about copayment percentages based on your taxable income threshold.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.3: This indicates the 20% copayment rate for standard income levels. This is informational only - your actual rate will be determined automatically based on your municipal tax records.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.4: This explains the special income threshold calculation. You don't need to fill anything out here - it's explaining how the 2.4 million yen total income limit affects deduction eligibility.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.5: This shows where to apply (ward office citizen general counter). Note the location for submitting your application - you'll need to go to your local ward office's citizen services counter.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.6: This is important: if the city confirms you qualify for the reduced copayment rate change, no application is needed. Check if you've already been notified of automatic qualification before applying.

_vision_explanations:Page 10 - Section 1.5: This is a notice stating that if the medical practitioner (like an acupuncturist or massage therapist) does not handle therapeutic fee reimbursement applications, you cannot use this particular reimbursement system. No action needed - this is just an important exclusion notice to be aware of before proceeding with your application.

_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.2: This refers to a cross-reference to page 20 of the document for specific instructions that apply to people under 70 years old and those between 70-75 years old. You should turn to page 20 to find the relevant income thresholds and procedures that determine your co-payment limits for medical expenses.

_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.1: This is an informational note explaining that the self-pay limit amounts shown are monthly limits, with amounts in angle brackets applying to frequent users (4+ times in 12 months). No action needed - just reference information.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.3: This shows the calculation formula for high-income earners (over 9.01 million yen): 252,600 yen plus 1% of medical costs exceeding 842,000 yen, with a reduced limit of 140,100 yen for frequent users. This is pre-calculated information, not a field to fill.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.4: This displays the formula for upper-middle income bracket (6-9.01 million yen): 167,400 yen plus 1% of costs over 558,000 yen, with 93,000 yen limit for frequent users. Reference only - no input required.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.6: Shows the calculation for middle-income bracket (2.1-6 million yen): 80,100 yen plus 1% of costs over 267,000 yen, with frequent user limit of 44,400 yen. This is reference information for understanding your coverage limits.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.7: Displays the flat monthly limit of 44,400 yen for low-income earners (under 2.1 million yen) and frequent users in higher brackets. No action needed - informational only.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.10: Same high-income calculation as field 3, repeated for the 70+ age group section. This is reference information showing your potential monthly limits based on income level.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.11: Identical to field 4 - shows upper-middle income calculation for seniors 70+. Use this to understand your coverage limits if your taxable income falls in the 6-9.01 million yen range.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.13: Same middle-income calculation as field 6, for the senior age group. Reference this if your income is 2.1-6 million yen to understand your monthly out-of-pocket limits.

_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 2.3: This is an informational note, not a field to fill out. It explains that if your total income exceeds 24 million yen, certain deduction amounts will be reduced or eliminated entirely - this mainly affects high earners and you don't need to write anything here.

_vision_explanations:Page 13 - Section 1.2: This section explains the meal therapy fee system for hospitalized patients. No action is required - it's informational about reduced meal costs available to low-income households and tax-exempt families with proper certification.
_vision_explanations:Page 13 - Section 1.5: This is a reference table showing standard meal costs per meal for different patient categories. No input needed - use this to understand your meal charges (490-510 yen for taxable households, lower rates for exempt households).
_vision_explanations:Page 13 - Section 1.12: This asks if you know about refill prescriptions, which can be reused up to 3 times to reduce medical costs. Simply read the information provided - no form filling required, just awareness of this cost-saving option.

_vision_explanations:Page 15 - Section 1.1: This is a header indicating the 'One-day Comprehensive Medical Checkup' program section. No action needed - this is just the title of the medical service being described.
_vision_explanations:Page 15 - Section 1.4: This is the contact phone number (043-238-9926) for inquiries about the one-day medical checkup program. Call this number if you have questions about eligibility or application procedures.

_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.2: This is an informational note, not a field to fill out. It tells you to check the list of participating medical institutions that comes with your examination voucher sticker to choose where to get your health checkup.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.3: This is a deadline reminder, not a fillable field. It informs you that you must complete your health examination by the end of February.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.4: This indicates the cost - you'll need to pay 500 yen when you receive your health checkup at the medical institution. This is not a field to fill out, just payment information.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.5: This describes outreach services provided by the program, not something you need to fill out. It explains that they will contact you through notifications and phone calls to encourage participation.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.6: This describes follow-up support services, not a fillable field. It explains that they provide treatment encouragement through phone calls and visits, particularly for diabetic nephropathy prevention.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.7: This simply means 'implementation' and refers to the execution of the health guidance programs described. It's not a field requiring your input.

_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 1.4: This is informational text explaining that long-term care insurance premiums for those under 65 are calculated monthly from the month before your birthday (or two months before if born on the 1st) and paid in 10 installments from June to March. No action is required from you - this is just explaining how the payment schedule works.

_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.4: This indicates that any calculated amounts should be rounded down to the nearest 10 yen (drop any amount less than 10 yen). This is a standard rounding rule for Japanese tax and insurance calculations.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.6: This is the medical care insurance premium rate of 7.14%. This percentage is typically pre-filled and used to calculate your medical insurance premiums based on your income.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.9: This shows the annual premium limit of 660,000 yen for medical care insurance. If your calculated premium exceeds this amount, it will be capped at this maximum.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.11: This is the long-term care support premium rate of 2.85%. This rate applies to calculate premiums for those who support the long-term care system (typically ages 40-64).
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.12: This shows the annual premium limit of 260,000 yen for long-term care support insurance. Your calculated premium cannot exceed this maximum amount.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.14: This is the nursing care insurance premium rate of 2.36%. This rate is used to calculate nursing care insurance premiums, typically for residents aged 65 and older.

_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.1: This is an informational note explaining that long-term care insurance premiums are calculated only for enrollees aged 40-64. No action needed - this is just explaining how the calculation works.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.2: This is another informational note stating that if the calculated amount exceeds the assessment limit, the limit amount becomes the final calculated amount. No input required - just explaining the calculation cap.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.3: This appears to be a section header explaining what the 'taxation assessment base amount' means. This is likely followed by explanatory text - no input needed from you.

_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 2.3: This is an instruction, not a field to fill - it tells you to round down any amounts under 100 yen when calculating. No action needed from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 2.4: This is explanatory text explaining that insurance premiums are calculated per individual then combined at household level. No input required from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 2.6: This is a footnote explaining that basic deductions decrease for high earners over 24 million yen annual income. This is informational only - no entry needed.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 2.9: This is mid-sentence explanatory text about how comprehensive and separate taxation portions are combined in calculations. This is instructional text only, requiring no input from you.

_vision_explanations:Page 19 - Section 1.2: This is an informational statement, not a field to fill out. It explains that if the recalculated insurance premium exceeds the amount you've already paid, you will need to pay the difference.

_vision_explanations:Page 20 - Section 1.1: This is informational text explaining that if you can't make a payment due to insufficient funds, the next month's insurance premium will be combined with the missed payment for a total of 2 months deducted together. No action needed from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 20 - Section 1.2: This is informational text explaining that payment vouchers for the full year (10 payments) will be sent to you in June each year. Keep these vouchers safe as you'll need them to make payments at banks or convenience stores.
_vision_explanations:Page 20 - Section 1.6: This lists financial institutions where you can set up automatic bank transfers for your pension payments. Choose one of these banks or credit unions if you want to use their services for automatic deduction.

_vision_explanations:Page 22 - Section 1.5: This field shows the standard amount (基準額) used to calculate insurance premium reductions for different household sizes. As a reference table, you don't need to fill this in - it displays the baseline amounts (127万円 for 1 person, 180万円 for 2 people, 220万円 for 3 people) that determine your eligibility for premium reductions.

_vision_explanations:Page 23 - Section 1.13: This is an informational note explaining that 'other temporary income' includes various types of income that can be used for daily living expenses, such as savings withdrawals, insurance payouts, retirement funds, annual payments from relatives or friends, child allowances, and remittances. No action is required for this field as it's just clarifying what types of income should be included in your calculations.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.1: This indicates the form type/number - you don't need to fill this in as it's pre-printed. This is Form No. 6 for National Health Insurance premium reduction applications.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.2: This appears to be a reference or control number field, likely pre-printed on the form. If it's blank, you may need to enter a case number or application ID provided by the government office, or leave it blank if this is your first submission.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.9: Enter the total amount of insurance premiums paid during the tax year (125,100 yen in this example). Include all eligible insurance premiums such as life insurance, medical insurance, and pension contributions.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.12: Enter the calculated deduction amount based on your insurance premium payments (45,100 yen in this example). This amount is typically less than the total premiums paid due to deduction limits set by tax law.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.7: Enter the monetary amount 35,100 yen as shown. This appears to be a pre-filled amount related to tax or fee calculations.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.10: Write '料を' (ryō wo) which means 'fee' or 'charge'. This is likely part of a standard phrase about payment of fees.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.11: Write '7月期（2期）' indicating the July period (2nd term). This refers to a specific tax payment period in the Japanese fiscal system.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.12: Enter the amount 10,000 yen. This appears to be another monetary figure, possibly related to the fee or tax calculation mentioned in the form.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.6: Write the amount in yen for this tax payment period, followed by 'R' to indicate it's been calculated. If you're unsure of the amount, check your tax assessment notice or consult the tax office.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.7: Enter the yen amount for this corresponding tax period, also followed by 'R'. This appears to be for a different payment period or tax category than field 6.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.10: This shows 35,100 yen as a pre-filled or calculated amount. If this is editable, verify it matches your tax assessment; if pre-filled, ensure it's correct for your situation.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.12: This indicates '1st period' (第1期) for tax payment scheduling. This is likely pre-printed and shows which installment period this form covers - typically tax payments are divided into multiple periods throughout the year.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.2: Enter the tax amount for the first payment period. This should match the amount shown on your tax notice from the municipality.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.4: This indicates the first payment period (typically April-June). Leave as-is unless your payment schedule differs.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.6: Enter the tax amount for the second payment period. Check your municipal tax notice for the correct amount.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.8: This indicates the second payment period (typically July-September). Leave as-is unless your payment schedule differs.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.10: This appears to be rejection text - leave blank unless you are rejecting/declining something specific on this form.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.12: This indicates the third payment period (typically October-December). Leave as-is unless your payment schedule differs.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.1: This appears to be part of a rejection notice template - you would not fill this out as an applicant, as it's used by officials to indicate application rejection.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.3: This refers to a 3-month period designation, likely for visa duration or reporting periods - check your specific form instructions to see if this applies to your situation.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.10: Select this option if you are the head of household/primary applicant filling out the form for yourself, rather than having someone else complete it on your behalf.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 8:.6: This is explanatory text about insurance premium amounts from your National Health Insurance notification - no need to fill anything here, just reference information.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 8:.7: Enter your phone number in the format shown (080-1234-5678). Use hyphens between the sections as demonstrated in the example.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 8:.11: This is explanatory text about payment due dates for National Health Insurance - no input required, it's just explaining where to find due date information on official notices.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 8:.13: Write the name of the person responsible or in charge of this matter. If filing for yourself, write your own name; if filing on behalf of someone else, write your name as the representative.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.12: Enter 1,500 yen or replace with your actual amount. Like field 7, this appears to be a sample amount that should be updated with your real figures.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.3: Enter your taxpayer identification number or reference number exactly as it appears on your tax documents. Include the hyphen and ensure all digits are correct as this is used to identify your account.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.4: Enter the total amount of income or deduction being reported in yen. Make sure this matches the amount shown in your source documents like tax withholding slips or receipts.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.9: Enter the same total amount as field 4 to confirm the calculation. Double-check that both amounts match exactly to avoid processing delays.

Enter the specific insurance type code or medical classification code as indicated on your insurance card or medical documents - if you're unsure of the exact code, consult with the office staff or your insurance provider.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.5: This is a monetary amount field showing 2,000 (likely yen). Enter the appropriate amount related to income, deductions, or payments as specified in the form instructions.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.3: Enter the amount of national health insurance premiums you paid during the tax year in yen. Keep records of your premium payments or check with your local municipal office to confirm the exact amount paid.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.2: Enter the amount of national health insurance premiums you paid during the year. Keep receipts from your city/ward office as documentation.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.12: Enter any tax refund amount you received during the tax year in parentheses. Leave blank if you didn't receive any refunds.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.5: Enter any deductions or adjustments in parentheses. This is likely a tax deduction amount that reduces your taxable income.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.6: Enter a secondary income or tax amount. This could be additional income from other sources or a specific tax calculation.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.9: Write the tax year in Japanese era format followed by month and day (3R6 = Reiwa 6th year, 4.1 = April 1st). Convert Western calendar dates to the Japanese era system.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.10: Enter another income or expense amount, likely related to the calculation above. Keep amounts consistent with your actual financial records.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.12: Indicate the payment period or installment number in parentheses. '2期' means '2nd period' - select the appropriate payment term for your situation.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 10.3: Enter your phone number in the standard Japanese format with hyphens, as shown in the example (080-1234-5678). Use your mobile or landline number where you can be reached.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 10.8: This explains the original due date for insurance premium payments as specified in your National Health Insurance notification under 'Regular Collection.' This is informational text, not a field you fill out.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 10.11: This appears to be part of a longer phrase about responsibility or being in charge of something. Without seeing the complete context, it's likely describing who is responsible for the insurance payments.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 10.12: This note explains that if the due date falls on a weekend or holiday, the payment deadline extends to the next business day. This is informational text explaining the payment schedule.

dfdccb85dd0d3302af05c087e3030552.note: This appears to be part of a larger field label - likely refers to documenting any physical injuries or disabilities. Leave blank if not applicable.

1175f89d8aac8fd1e17a37f8126d0890.en:
I notice there seems to be an error in your request. The text you've provided "and rectum" appears to be in English already, not Japanese. This text also doesn't make sense in the context of a municipal residence registration form (住民異動届).

Could you please provide the actual Japanese text that appears on the form? Japanese government forms typically use Japanese characters (hiragana, katakana, and/or kanji).

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.1: This is just the title of the medical classification table - no action needed from you as it's a header explaining what the document contains.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.3: This is a category header for blood and blood-forming organ diseases - you don't need to fill anything here, it's just organizational labeling.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.4: This continues the category description for immune system disorders - again, this is just a classification label, not a field to complete.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.5: This refers to sexually transmitted infections classification code - only relevant if you're reporting a medical diagnosis in this category.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.7: This completes the STI classification description - no input required as it's part of the medical coding system.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.8: This is the Japanese translation of the STI category - purely informational text that doesn't require any response from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.9: This is a section header for endocrine, nutritional and metabolic disorders - no action needed, just organizational labeling.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.10: This is a classification code for viral skin infections - only relevant if reporting a specific medical condition in this category.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.11: This completes the viral infection description - informational text only, no input required from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.12: This refers to long-term effects of infectious diseases classification - only relevant if you have such medical history to report.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.13: This is a classification for dementia types - only relevant if reporting or dealing with dementia-related medical issues.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.14: This refers to mental/behavioral disorders from substance use - only relevant if reporting related medical conditions.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.15: This completes the substance use disorder classification - informational text that doesn't require any input from you.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.1: Check this box if the person had liver cancer or cancer of the bile ducts inside the liver. This includes primary liver cancer (hepatocellular carcinoma) and cholangiocarcinoma.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.2: This is a continuation of field 1 - it refers to the bile ducts portion of liver and intrahepatic bile duct cancers. Only check if specifically diagnosed with bile duct cancer.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.3: Check this box if the person had cancer of the windpipe (trachea), main breathing tubes (bronchus), or lungs. This covers most respiratory tract cancers.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.4: This is a continuation of field 3 referring specifically to lung cancer. Check if the person was diagnosed with any type of lung cancer.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.5: Check this box if the person was diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease, a progressive brain disorder that affects memory and thinking abilities.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.6: Check this box if the person had cerebral palsy (brain damage affecting movement) or other conditions causing paralysis or movement difficulties.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.7: Check this box if the person had disorders affecting automatic body functions like heart rate, blood pressure, or digestion due to nervous system problems.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.1: Check this box if you have other spinal disorders not specifically listed elsewhere, such as chronic back pain, spinal stenosis, or other vertebral conditions diagnosed by a doctor.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.2: This refers to abnormal test results from blood work, urine tests, or other laboratory studies that don't fit into other specific disease categories - consult the full classification list if you have unexplained abnormal lab values.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.3: Select this if you have bone density problems like osteoporosis, osteopenia, or structural bone disorders that affect bone strength or architecture as diagnosed through bone density scans or medical examination.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.5: Choose this category for general symptoms, physical signs, or abnormal clinical findings that don't clearly fit into specific disease classifications - this is a broad category for various health indicators.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.6: Same as item 2 - this covers abnormal laboratory test results (blood, urine, imaging, etc.) that indicate health issues but don't correspond to a specific disease category in the classification system.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.1: This is a category header for symptoms and abnormal findings that don't fit elsewhere in the medical classification system - you would select this if your condition involves unexplained symptoms or test results.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.2: This refers to abnormal lab test results or clinical findings that can't be classified under specific diseases - check this if you have concerning test results but no definitive diagnosis.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.4: Select this category for any conditions affecting kidneys, bladder, reproductive organs, or urinary system - it's a broad category covering genitourinary health issues.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.6: Choose this section for injuries, poisoning, or external causes of harm - it covers trauma, accidents, toxic exposures, and their medical consequences.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.7: Select this specific code if you have brain injuries, skull fractures, or damage to internal organs from trauma - it's for serious internal injuries requiring medical attention.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.8: Choose this for kidney stones, bladder problems, or other urinary system diseases not specifically listed elsewhere - it's a catch-all for unlisted urinary conditions.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.9: Select this for male reproductive system issues not specifically listed, such as certain prostate problems or testicular conditions - it covers miscellaneous male genital health issues.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.11: This is an important notice that medical code 1503 (marked with *) is not covered by Japan's National Health Insurance - you would pay full cost for treatment under this code.

_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.1: Check this if you have vision problems like nearsightedness, farsightedness, astigmatism, or need glasses/contacts. This is one of the most common eye conditions.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.2: Mark this for common upper respiratory infections like colds, sinus infections, or throat infections that aren't specifically flu or COVID-19.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.3: Select this for outer ear problems like ear infections, earwax blockage, or external ear canal issues that cause pain or hearing problems.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.4: Choose this for persistent cough or chest congestion when you're unsure if it's acute (short-term) or chronic (long-term) bronchitis.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.5: Mark this for middle ear infections, eardrum problems, or mastoid bone infections that typically cause ear pain and hearing issues.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.6: Select this for chronic lung conditions like COPD, emphysema, or chronic bronchitis that cause ongoing breathing difficulties.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.7: This is a category header for heart and blood vessel diseases. You would check specific conditions listed under this section, not this header itself.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.8: Check this if you have gum disease, bleeding gums, or periodontal problems that affect the tissues around your teeth.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.9: Mark this for stomach inflammation (gastritis) or small intestine inflammation (duodenitis) causing stomach pain or digestive issues.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.10: Select this for stroke or blocked arteries in the brain/neck area. This is a serious condition requiring immediate medical attention.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.11: Choose this for long-term liver inflammation not caused by alcohol or other specified causes, often requiring ongoing medical management.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.12: Mark this for other brain blood vessel problems like small strokes, vascular dementia, or cerebral bleeding not covered by other categories.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.14: Check this if you have chronically low blood pressure that causes symptoms like dizziness, fainting, or fatigue.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.1: This is just a header text meaning 'Supporting everyone's daily life' - no writing required, it's part of the document title.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.3: This shows 'Matsudo City' as the issuing municipality - no action needed, this identifies which city government published this guide.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.4: This is a warning notice about phone scams impersonating city employees - read this carefully but no writing required, it's alerting you to be cautious of fraudulent calls.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.6: This states that all insurance premium refunds are processed through written documents only - this is informational text explaining the city's official policy, no action needed from you.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.1: This is just a table of contents header - no action needed from you as this is informational only.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.2: This appears to be a dotted line for formatting - no information needs to be filled in here.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.3: This is a section header about the National Health Insurance system - no input required, just informational.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.4: Another formatting line with dots - no information needs to be entered here.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.6: This appears to be a dotted line for page formatting - no action required from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.7: Another formatting element with dots - no information needs to be filled in.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.8: This is a dotted line for formatting purposes - no input needed from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.9: Another formatting line - no information required to be entered here.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.10: This appears to be a dotted formatting line - no action needed from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.11: Another dotted line for formatting - no information needs to be filled in.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.12: This is an informational header about medical benefits when receiving treatment - no input required, just read for understanding.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.13: This appears to be a dotted line for formatting - no information needs to be entered.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.14: Another formatting element with dots - no action required from you.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.1: Write the lump-sum amount received when giving birth (maternity allowance). This is typically around 420,000 yen from health insurance.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.2: Enter the lump-sum amount received when someone in your household dies (funeral allowance). This is usually around 50,000 yen from health insurance.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.3: Write the amount received for funeral expenses. Keep receipts as documentation for this deduction.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.4: Enter the total amount spent on home nursing care services. This includes services from visiting care stations and is tax-deductible.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.5: Write the amount of medical expenses not covered by national health insurance. This applies when you cannot use your insurance card.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.6: Enter high medical expenses for people under 70 years old. This is for the high-cost medical expense system.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.7: Write high medical expenses for people aged 70-74. Age verification may be required for this category.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.8: Enter the amount when you've had 4 or more instances of high medical expenses in a year. This triggers additional reimbursement.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.10: Write the total when both someone under 70 and someone aged 70-74 in the same household have high medical expenses.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.11: Enter expenses under the annual high medical expense system for outpatient care. This is calculated yearly rather than monthly.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.13: Write expenses under the high medical/nursing care combined system. This combines both medical and nursing care costs.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.14: Enter medical expenses for people aged 75 and older. This is for the elderly medical care system.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 2:.15: Write the total combined medical and nursing care expenses. This is for the integrated expense calculation system.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 3:.1: Write the specific disease name designated by the Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare that qualifies for special medical coverage.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 3:.2: Enter the income classification category that determines your medical cost burden level and copayment amount.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 3:.3: Write 'Yes' or check if you can save on medical expenses through this special disease designation system.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 3:.4: Enter details about specific health checkups and health guidance you receive related to your designated disease.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 3:.5: Write information about medical cost notifications you receive from your insurance provider.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 3:.6: Enter details about the Late-Stage Elderly Medical Care System if you're 75 or older (or 65-74 with certain conditions).
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 3:.7: Write the specific situations when you must submit documents within 14 days, such as changes in income or medical condition.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 3:.8: This refers to the back cover of the form - no need to write anything here.

_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.3: This indicates the flow of medical treatment in the Japanese healthcare system - no action required from you as this is explanatory text showing how medical services are provided.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.10: This section is about organ donation consent and is completely optional (任意 means voluntary). You can leave this blank or indicate your preference regarding organ donation if you choose to participate in Japan's organ donation program.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.11: This provides contact information for the Japan Organ Transplant Network if you have questions about organ donation. No action needed unless you want to contact them at the provided phone number 0120-78-1069.

_vision_explanations:Page 4 - Section 1:.7: This indicates when you move out to another municipality. You need to notify within 14 days of your moving date to stop your national health insurance coverage.
_vision_explanations:Page 4 - Section 1:.11: This explains that if you don't submit the discontinuation notice, insurance premiums will continue to be charged even after moving. Make sure to complete the proper paperwork to avoid double payments.

_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 1:.14: This indicates that preschool children (under 6 years old) receive a discount on the per-capita portion of health insurance premiums. If you have children under 6, their per-capita levy will be reduced - this is automatically calculated by the system, so you don't need to write anything here.

_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 1:.1: This text states that the amount becomes half price, indicating a 50% discount applies. This appears to be informational text explaining that certain fees or charges are reduced by half when the 平等割 (equal allocation) discount is applied - no action needed from you as this is just explaining the benefit.

_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 2:.4: This is informational text explaining that you don't need to pay beyond the maximum limits shown. No action required - this is just clarifying that the three insurance categories (medical, elderly support, and long-term care) each have annual payment caps, so you won't be charged above these amounts.

_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.3: This is showing the mathematical formula for how insurance premiums are calculated - you don't need to write anything here, it's just explaining that your total premium equals medical insurance portion plus long-term care support portion.
_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.6: This is explanatory text in a speech bubble showing understanding of the payment structure - no action required, it's just confirming that you understand both portions need to be paid together.

_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.1: This is informational text stating that continued non-payment of insurance premiums will result in the following measures. No input required - this is just explaining consequences.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.3: This is informational text explaining that property seizure is legally mandated. No input required - this describes legal requirements for asset seizure.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.4: This is informational text about premium payment obligations and property investigations. No input required - this explains the process of debt collection procedures.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.6: This is informational text explaining transfer to debt collection specialists when payment is unlikely. No input required - this describes escalation procedures.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.7: This is informational text about transferring cases to the 'Debt Management Division'. No input required - this explains the final collection stage.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.9: This refers to accessing the Matsudo City Homepage for more information. Visit the city website if you need additional details about procedures.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.11: This indicates guidance/information section. No input required - this is just a section header directing you to available resources.

_vision_explanations:Page 9 - Section 1:.2: This is an informational section, not a field to fill out. It explains the types of medical care covered by National Health Insurance (diagnosis, treatment, medication, hospitalization, home medical care, and visiting nursing care) and shows the co-payment percentages based on age - 20% for children and elderly 70-74, 30% for working-age adults 6-69, with special provisions for high earners aged 70-75.

_vision_explanations:Page 10 - Section 1.4: This checkbox is for situations where crop failure due to drought, cold weather damage, or other agricultural disasters has significantly reduced your income. Only select this if you are a farmer or agricultural worker whose income has been substantially affected by natural disasters that damaged crops.

_vision_explanations:Page 10 - Section 1.1: This is a statement declaring that you will pay the standard amount for room and board fees as determined by the facility. This appears to be a declaration clause that you would initial, sign, or check to acknowledge your agreement to pay these standardized costs during your stay at a medical or care facility.

_vision_explanations:Page 10 - Section 2.1: This is informational text explaining that your daily burden amount includes the standard meal cost plus 370 yen residence fee (0 yen for designated intractable diseases). No action needed - this is just explaining how costs are calculated.
_vision_explanations:Page 10 - Section 2.2: This is a notice stating that meal and accommodation fees paid during hospitalization are not eligible for high-cost medical care benefits. No input required - this is informational only.

_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.1: This is a header explaining the reimbursement process - when you pay full medical costs upfront, you can later apply to National Health Insurance for partial reimbursement of your out-of-pocket expenses.
_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.2: This describes scenario 4 for reimbursement - when you receive treatment from an unlicensed judo therapist/bonesetter for bone fractures, sprains, etc. without using your insurance card initially.
_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.4: This refers to receiving judo therapy/chiropractic treatment from practitioners who don't have proper licensing or aren't covered by insurance - you can apply for reimbursement if you paid out of pocket.
_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.7: This introduces scenario 5 for medical expense reimbursement - specifically for overseas medical treatment when you become ill while traveling abroad.
_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.9: This clarifies that medical equipment like corsets must be deemed medically necessary by a doctor for treatment purposes to qualify for reimbursement.
_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.10: This refers to receiving emergency medical treatment at overseas medical facilities when you suddenly become ill during international travel.
_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.11: This is the category name for overseas medical expense reimbursement - note that long-term overseas residents and those traveling specifically for medical treatment are excluded from this benefit.

_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.2: This is informational text explaining that childbirth lump-sum benefits are paid when a member gives birth. No action needed - this is just explaining the benefit amount of 488,000 yen.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.6: This is additional information noting that if you give birth at a medical institution enrolled in the obstetric medical compensation system, the benefit amount is 500,000 yen instead. No input required - just informational text.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.7: This clarifies that the benefit amounts (488,000 or 500,000 yen) apply to births from April 1, 2023 onwards, with amounts varying by birth date. This is informational only - no form input needed.

_vision_explanations:Page 14 - Section 1.1: This explains that amounts exceeding the limit are paid as 'high-cost medical care benefits,' with limits varying by age and income bracket. No action needed - this is informational text.
_vision_explanations:Page 14 - Section 1.2: This column shows the maximum out-of-pocket amount you'll pay for medical expenses based on your income level. Find your income bracket and note the corresponding limit formula.
_vision_explanations:Page 14 - Section 1.3: This column shows reduced limits that apply from your 4th hospitalization onward in a 12-month period. The amounts are lower than the regular limits to reduce burden for frequent users.
_vision_explanations:Page 14 - Section 1.6: If your annual income is 6-9 million yen, this formula calculates your limit: 167,400 yen plus 1% of medical costs exceeding 558,000 yen. The system will automatically calculate this based on your actual medical expenses.
_vision_explanations:Page 14 - Section 1.7: For income bracket 'イ' (6-9 million yen annually), your maximum monthly out-of-pocket from the 4th time onward is capped at 93,000 yen. This provides additional relief for repeat users.
_vision_explanations:Page 14 - Section 1.8: If your annual income is 2.1-6 million yen, this formula applies: 80,100 yen plus 1% of medical costs over 267,000 yen. Most middle-income residents fall into this category.
_vision_explanations:Page 14 - Section 1.12: Important warning: If anyone in your household hasn't filed a tax return, you'll automatically be placed in the highest income bracket 'ア'. Ensure all household members file tax returns even if they have no income to avoid higher medical costs.

_vision_explanations:Page 14 - Section 1.1: This is an informational note explaining that even within the same hospital or medical facility, you must calculate medical costs separately for different types of care - medical vs dental, and inpatient vs outpatient services. Each type of care gets its own medical fee statement, so track and calculate the costs for each category independently when determining if you've reached the threshold amounts.
_vision_explanations:Page 14 - Section 1.2: This note explains that if multiple family members in your household each have out-of-pocket medical expenses of 21,000 yen or more, you should add all of these qualifying amounts together when calculating your total medical deduction. Only include expenses of 21,000 yen or higher per person - smaller amounts don't count toward the combined total.

_vision_explanations:Page 15 - Section 1.4: This shows the calculation formula for high-income earners (Class III) - you don't write anything here, it's just showing how your limit is calculated based on total medical costs.
_vision_explanations:Page 15 - Section 1.5: This indicates the reduced limit (140,100 yen) that applies from your 4th high-cost medical treatment within 12 months - no action needed, just for reference.
_vision_explanations:Page 15 - Section 1.6: This shows the reduced limit (93,000 yen) for Class II income earners from their 4th treatment onward - informational only.
_vision_explanations:Page 15 - Section 1.7: This displays the reduced limit (44,400 yen) for Class I income earners and general category from the 4th treatment - no input required.
_vision_explanations:Page 15 - Section 1.8: This indicates the annual spending cap (144,000 yen) for general category patients - you don't need to write anything here.
_vision_explanations:Page 15 - Section 1.14: This note explains that non-taxpaying households with hospital stays over 91 days in 12 months need separate application - check if this applies to you and file additional paperwork if needed.

_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.2: This explains that application forms will be sent to eligible households - no action needed here, just read the instructions about bringing required documents when the form arrives.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.7: Calculate the limit amount for household members aged 70-75 using the reference table on page 15 - you'll need to look up the income bracket and corresponding limit amount.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.8: Add any out-of-pocket medical expenses of 21,000 yen or more paid by household members under 70 to the limit amount you calculated in step 7 above.

_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 1.2: This is informational text explaining that people who don't file tax returns are still considered high-income earners. No action needed - this is just clarifying the definition.
_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 1.6: This explains that you can apply to be treated as 'general' category instead of the current income category. If you meet certain conditions and want lower healthcare premiums, you may want to apply for reclassification.
_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 1.14: This defines low-income category I as people whose income becomes 0 yen after all deductions. This is informational text explaining eligibility - no writing required, just check if your situation matches this description.

_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 1.3: This is informational text explaining that generic drugs are made with the same active ingredients as brand-name drugs after patents expire. No writing required - this is educational content about cost-saving options.
_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 1.5: This is a header encouraging self-medication practices. No writing required - this introduces the next section about managing minor health issues independently.
_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 1.6: This is a subheading that will be followed by the definition of self-medication. No writing required - this is just a section label for informational content.

_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 2.1: This is a heading section - no writing required. It's encouraging you to practice self-medication as a health management approach.
_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 2.2: This is a definition header - no writing required. It introduces the explanation of what self-medication means.
_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 2.3: This is explanatory text about using over-the-counter medicines for self-care - no writing required. It's part of the informational content about self-medication practices.
_vision_explanations:Page 17 - Section 2.4: This is continuing explanatory text about cost savings and chronic conditions - no writing required. It's part of the educational content explaining when self-medication is appropriate.

_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.2: This is explanatory text stating that health guidance will be provided - no action needed from you as this describes what the city will do.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.3: This is informational text explaining that eligible persons will receive notifications after health checkups - no input required from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.4: This completes the sentence about sending notifications - no action needed as this is just explanatory text.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.8: This is a header for the 'Severe Disease Prevention Program' section - no input required, just informational.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.10: This indicates that a registered dietitian is part of the healthcare team - no action needed, this is just showing available professional support.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.11: This is explanatory text about health checkup screening for diabetic nephropathy or CKD - no input required from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.12: This continues the explanation about chronic kidney disease risk assessment - no action needed, just informational text.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.13: This describes people who received appropriate medical treatment or health guidance - no input required from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.14: This explains what Matsudo City provides to ensure residents can receive proper guidance - no action needed, just informational.

_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.2: This is explanatory text stating that the national health insurance supports enrollees - no action needed from you as it's informational content.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.3: This is a bullet point recommending you receive annual specific health checkups and monitor your test results - it's guidance text, not a field to fill out.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.5: This refers to submitting results from workplace health examinations you may have received at your job - check if your employer provides annual health checkups that you can submit to your insurance.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 1.6: This indicates cost assistance available for comprehensive medical examinations (human dock) - inquire with your local office about subsidy programs for preventive health screenings.

_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 2.1: This section explains that if you submit your workplace health examination results, it will be considered equivalent to receiving the municipal health screening. You don't need to write anything here - it's informational text about using workplace health check results instead of municipal screening.
_vision_explanations:Page 18 - Section 2.2: This is informational text about subsidy programs available for those who pay for private comprehensive health checks (human dock) or medical examinations. No action required - just note that details about financial assistance can be found in the booklet enclosed with your health screening voucher.

_vision_explanations:Page 20 - Section 1.2: This section lists various life situations when you need to submit this form within 14 days, such as moving from another municipality, changing health insurance, having a child, or starting/stopping welfare benefits. You don't need to write anything here - just find the situation that matches your circumstances and prepare the required documents listed in the right column.
This is informational text, not a field to fill out. It explains that this is a system allowing 2 years of enrollment in health insurance continuation after retirement.
_vision_explanations:Page 20 - Section 1.3: This is a footer note about environmental printing practices, not a field requiring any input from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 20 - Section 1.4: This is a copyright notice for the publication. No action needed - it's just legal text protecting the publisher's rights.
